subject: Physics
prerequisites: Measuring length, time and mass and using SI units
Rearranging a simple equation and substituting values
Plotting points on, and reading values from, a graph

learning_objectives.statement: Define speed and velocity, calculate average speed using average speed = distance / time, and distinguish speed (a scalar) from velocity (speed in a stated direction, a vector).
Plot and interpret distance-time and speed-time graphs: the gradient of a distance-time graph is speed and the gradient of a speed-time graph is acceleration.

Calculate acceleration from the gradient of a speed-time graph and the distance travelled from the area under it for uniformly accelerated motion, and recall that the acceleration of free fall g is about 9.8 m/s^2 near the Earth's surface.
Distinguish mass (the quantity of matter, in kg) from weight (the gravitational force on an object, in N) and calculate weight using W = mg with gravitational field strength g = 9.8 N/kg.

Describe how a resultant force can change the speed, direction or shape of an object, find the resultant of forces acting along the same straight line, and describe friction as a force that opposes motion.

State and apply Newton's second law as resultant force = mass x acceleration (F = ma) for motion in a straight line, and use Newton's first law to explain motion at constant velocity when forces are balanced.

depth_profile: Cambridge IGCSE (0625) rigour, Core with the Supplement extension flagged. Students define and use speed, velocity and acceleration with average speed = distance / time; read and interpret distance-time and speed-time graphs (gradient, and area for Supplement); distinguish mass and weight and use W = mg; find the resultant of forces along a line; and state and apply F = ma to straight-line motion. Keep to one- and two-step numerical problems with quantities given, qualitative reasoning about balanced versus unbalanced forces, and correct SI units. Do NOT use calculus, resolution of forces into components, inclined-plane force triangles, or momentum - those are beyond this topic. Supplement objectives (coded S) go one step further (area under a speed-time graph, acceleration of free fall); Core objectives (coded C) are required of every candidate.
assessment_notes: Cambridge IGCSE Physics is examined as Core (grades C-G) or Extended (Core plus Supplement, grades A*-E); use the Core/Supplement tags to calibrate depth. Questions are multiple-choice and short structured items. Marks are awarded for selecting the correct equation, substituting correctly, and stating the answer with the correct SI unit - a missing or wrong unit commonly costs the final mark. Values such as g are given on the paper. Graph questions reward a correct gradient or area method with working shown. Command words ('state', 'describe', 'calculate', 'determine') set the expected response.
reference_data: Use these values consistently in EVERY worked example and practice question; do not substitute other figures: acceleration of free fall g = 9.8 m/s^2; gravitational field strength g = 9.8 N/kg. VALIDATE against the current Cambridge IGCSE Physics (0625) formula/data sheet before classroom use; some past papers use g = 10, so this table only guarantees that all sections use the SAME value.
source: Hand-seeded for POC - validate against the Cambridge IGCSE Physics (0625) syllabus 2023-2025, Topic 1 (Motion, forces and energy).

exam_map.value: Speed / velocity / acceleration from **graphs**, F = ma, W = mg, and resultant forces
Read **gradients** (and areas, Supplement) off distance–time and speed–time graphs
**W = mg** and stating Newton's first law for constant velocity are reliable marks

spec_source_citation: Cambridge IGCSE Physics (0625) syllabus 2023–2025, Topic 1 (validate against the official syllabus).

spec_checklist.can_do: I can define speed and velocity, calculate average speed using average speed = distance / time, and distinguish speed (a scalar) from velocity (speed in a stated direction, a vector).
I can plot and interpret distance-time and speed-time graphs: the gradient of a distance-time graph is speed and the gradient of a speed-time graph is acceleration.

I can calculate acceleration from the gradient of a speed-time graph and the distance travelled from the area under it for uniformly accelerated motion, and recall that the acceleration of free fall g is about 9.8 m/s^2 near the Earth's surface.
I can distinguish mass (the quantity of matter, in kg) from weight (the gravitational force on an object, in N) and calculate weight using W = mg with gravitational field strength g = 9.8 N/kg.

I can describe how a resultant force can change the speed, direction or shape of an object, find the resultant of forces acting along the same straight line, and describe friction as a force that opposes motion.

I can state and apply Newton's second law as resultant force = mass x acceleration (F = ma) for motion in a straight line, and use Newton's first law to explain motion at constant velocity when forces are balanced.
